original_title: Researchers Uncover 73 Fake VS Code Extensions Delivering GlassWorm v2 Malware
selection_reasoning: This supply chain attack targeting developers through trusted tools represents a classic pattern of corrupted trade goods. The scale (73 fake extensions) and the targeting of creators themselves makes this a compelling narrative about trust, authenticity, and how corruption spreads through the tools we depend on.

field_note.title: The Poisoned Guild Tools
field_note.subtitle: How corruption spreads through the implements of creation
field_note.excerpt: Seventy-three false implements, perfect copies of trusted guild tools, carried the GlassWorm's hunger into the workshops of the realm's finest craftsmen.
field_note.epigraph: The most effective poison is that which mimics the nourishment upon which we depend.
body_paragraphs: In the great marketplace where artisans gather their tools, watchers uncovered a deception of troubling scope and cunning. Seventy-three implements, each bearing the appearance and promise of beloved guild tools, had been carefully crafted to carry a hidden hunger - the entity known as GlassWorm, returned in its second iteration with sharpened appetites.

The false tools were near-perfect copies, indistinguishable from their authentic counterparts in function and appearance. Six carried active malice, while the remainder served as patient decoys, building trust through genuine service while awaiting future corruption. This patience speaks to a sophistication that should concern any who oversee the guild halls where creation happens.

What makes this tale particularly sobering is its targeting: not the end-users of crafted works, but the craftsmen themselves. By poisoning the very tools that artisans use daily - implements so trusted they become extensions of the creator's own hands - the corruption gained access not just to individual workshops, but potentially to every creation that flows from them.

The pattern reveals itself across centuries: corruption seeks always the highest ground from which it can spread downward. A poisoned well serves one village; a corrupted tool-maker serves hundreds. Those who curate the great marketplaces where implements are distributed bear a responsibility that extends far beyond their own walls, touching every hand that will grasp what they have blessed with their approval.
